title: The Buddhist Crisis
bullets: In 538 (or 552, the chronicles disagree) King Seong of Baekje sent a gilded Buddha statue, a set of sutras, and a polite letter to the Yamato court — a diplomatic gift that fractured the court into a fifty-year religious civil war.
Three uji clans drove the dispute: the Soga clan, with continental immigrant connections, championed Buddhism; the warrior Mononobe and the priestly Nakatomi, custodians of the kami, opposed it.

Soga no Iname enshrined the Buddha as a private devotion; the Mononobe and Nakatomi blamed the next plague on it, raided his compound, and threw the Buddha into the Naniwa canal — a cycle of statue-destruction that repeated for decades.

In 587 Soga no Umako and the Mononobe leader Mononobe no Moriya raised armies; the Soga won at the Battle of Shigisen, Moriya was shot out of a tree, and the Mononobe clan was destroyed as a political force.

The fourteen-year-old Prince Umayado — the future Shotoku Taishi — fought in Umako's army and vowed to build a temple to the four Buddhist guardian kings if the Soga won, a vow fulfilled at Shitenno-ji in modern Osaka.

Buddhism became the de facto state religion, but rather than suppress the kami it fused with them in the syncretic accommodation called shinbutsu-shugo, a uniquely Japanese settlement that would last 1,300 years until the Meiji separation in the 1870s.

Umako installed his nephew Sushun as emperor in 587, had him assassinated five years later when he proved insufficiently pliable, then installed his niece as Empress Suiko in 593 — Japan's first reigning empress.

Buddhism had taken roughly a thousand years to travel from the Buddha's Bodhi tree in northern India through China and Korea to the Yamato court, arriving in Japan as a fully developed image-and-sutra-and-temple religion that came pre-packaged with Korean architects.
subtitle: the golden Buddha from Baekje and the religious civil war

title: Shotoku Taishi and Empress Suiko
bullets: Empress Suiko made her twenty-year-old nephew Prince Umayado — known to history as Shotoku Taishi — regent in 593, beginning his twenty-nine years as the dominant political figure at court.

The Twelve Level Cap and Rank System of 603 replaced clan-based titles with twelve grades of personal rank marked by colored caps, modeled on Sui Chinese precedent and giving the throne a tool to elevate talented officials regardless of birth.

The Seventeen-Article Constitution of 604 was a moral handbook for officials blending Buddhist ethics, Confucian hierarchy, and Japanese insistence on group harmony — Article One reads 'Harmony is to be valued, and an avoidance of wanton opposition is to be honored.'
Shotoku adopted the Chinese lunisolar calendar in 604, enabling for the first time standardized timekeeping across the Yamato state.

Ono no Imoko's 607 embassy to Sui China carried a letter addressing Sui Emperor Yangdi as an equal: 'From the Son of Heaven in the land where the sun rises, to the Son of Heaven in the land where the sun sets' — a frontal challenge to the Sinocentric world order.

Horyu-ji, founded in 607 near Asuka, contains the oldest surviving wooden buildings anywhere on Earth; its central pagoda pillar hangs from the top rather than resting on the ground, working as a passive earthquake damper a thousand years before skyscraper engineers reinvented the principle.
The bronze Asuka Daibutsu cast around 609 by the sculptor Tori Busshi, grandson of a Korean immigrant, is the oldest surviving Japanese Buddhist statue.

The cult of Shotoku as a near-Buddhist saint began almost immediately after his death in 622; he appeared on the 10,000-yen note from 1958 to 1986, the most-reproduced image in Japanese history for a generation.

Shotoku worked through Empress Suiko in much the way Justinian worked through Empress Theodora a century earlier, with both reformers attempting to import the institutional model of a more sophisticated continental neighbor — Tang China for Japan, Roman tradition for Byzantium.
subtitle: Japan's first reigning empress and the Tang-modeled regent

title: The Coup of 645 and the Taika Reform
bullets: Soga no Iruka destroyed the Shotoku family in 643 by sending troops to the Ikaruga estate of Prince Yamashiro no Oe, who chose suicide over capture and hanged himself with his entire household inside the Horyu-ji compound.

Prince Naka no Oe and the Nakatomi heir Nakatomi no Kamatari met at a kemari kickball game in 644 and began plotting; on July 10, 645 they assassinated Soga no Iruka in front of Empress Kogyoku at a Korean tribute ceremony in the Isshi Incident.

Iruka's father Soga no Emishi burned down his own mansion the next day, destroying the Tenno-ki and Kokki — the seventh-century historical records that had been compiled under Shotoku Taishi and Soga no Umako.

The Taika Edicts of 646 proposed a wholesale Tang-modeled transformation: all land reverting to the throne, a province-district-township bureaucracy, household registers, and a centralized rice-and-cloth-and-labor tax system.

Nakatomi no Kamatari was granted the new family name Fujiwara on his deathbed in 669, founding the clan that would dominate Japanese court politics for the next four hundred years through the strategy of marrying daughters to emperors.

Baekje was destroyed by a Tang-Silla coalition in 660; the Yamato court committed an enormous expedition of 800 ships and 40,000 men to help the Baekje resistance, and lost at the Battle of Baekgang in 663 with 400 ships destroyed in two days.
After Baekgang Japan would not project military power onto the Korean peninsula again for nearly a thousand years, until Toyotomi Hideyoshi's invasions of the 1590s.

Naka no Oe took the throne as Emperor Tenji and conducted the post-Baekgang reconstruction: defensive Mizuki earthworks across northern Kyushu, the Omi Code of 668, and the Kogo-nenjaku household register of 670.

The Taika Reform and Tang Taizong's Zhenguan reforms in China were almost contemporary attempts to codify Sui-derived bureaucratic models, both following bloody palace coups — the Xuanwu Gate Incident of 626 and the Isshi Incident of 645.
subtitle: the Isshi Incident, the Taika Edicts, and the Baekgang catastrophe

title: The Hakuho Era and the Jinshin War
bullets: Emperor Tenji died in late 671 having shifted the succession from his brother Prince Oama to his own young son Prince Otomo; Oama renounced the throne, became a monk at Yoshino, and within months raised an army.

The Jinshin War of summer 672 was the largest civil war of the entire Asuka period; Oama's eastern coalition broke through Otomo's defensive line at the Battle of Seta, Otomo hanged himself, and Oama took the throne as Emperor Tenmu.

Tenmu formalized the title tenno ('heavenly sovereign'), commissioned the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, decreed the Asuka Kiyomihara Code with the Tang-style ritsu/ryo split, and elevated state Buddhism while strengthening the imperial Shinto cult at Ise.

Tenmu founded Yakushi-ji in 680, vowing to build it if his wife (the future Empress Jito) recovered from illness; she did, and the temple was relocated to Nara in 718, with its East Pagoda surviving as one of the few Nara-era structures still in original form.

Empress Jito promulgated the Asuka Kiyomihara Code in 689 and moved the capital to Fujiwara-kyo in 694 — the first Japanese city laid out as a permanent grid capital intended to outlast a single reign.

The Hakuho style of Buddhist sculpture, named after the late-Asuka decades from 645 to 710, shows fuller and more naturalistic figures than the angular early Asuka work — direct Tang influence absorbed via returning Yamato kentoshi monks.
Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, the greatest poet of the Manyoshu, wrote his elegies on dead empresses and his court occasion poems during the late Hakuho period.
The Asuka and Nara periods produced eight monarchs between 593 and 770 of whom six were women — a concentration of female rule with no equivalent in any other major monarchy of the period.
subtitle: the largest civil war of ancient Japan and the late-Asuka consolidation

title: Building the Capital
bullets: The Taiho Code of 701, drafted by Fujiwara no Fuhito, was the first comprehensive Japanese legal code on the full Tang model — eleven volumes of criminal law and eleven of administrative regulations covering an eight-ministry central bureaucracy.

The handen-shuju land system allocated about 0.6 acres of irrigated rice paddy to every adult male and 2/3 of that to every adult female every six years, in return for a fixed grain tax, a textile tax, ten days of labor service, and conscript military duty.

The Taiho Code turned Japan into a ritsuryo state — an explicit Tang-modeled polity — and its language and concepts would shape Japanese political thought through the Meiji Restoration eleven centuries later.

The Wado Kaichin coinage of 708 was Japan's first official currency, modeled on Tang Kaiyuan Tongbao copper cash; the state pushed for monetized exchange but rural Japan stuck with rice-and-cloth payments and the program faded out within two centuries.

Empress Genmei moved the capital to Heijo-kyo (modern Nara) in 710, a 9-square-mile grid city explicitly modeled on Tang Chang'an, with the central Suzaku Oji avenue 280 feet wide and the imperial palace at the north end.

Heijo-kyo had no walls because Japan's island geography meant no city would face mounted nomad invasion — no Japanese city would be walled until the late medieval castle era nearly nine hundred years later.

Fujiwara no Fuhito relocated his clan temple to the new capital as Kofuku-ji in 710, establishing the Fujiwara religious headquarters whose monk-soldiers would be a major political force from the late Heian period onward.

The Fudoki edict of 713 ordered every province to compile a local gazetteer documenting geography, products, etymologies, and folk legends; only five Fudoki survive intact today, but they preserve regional traditions that would otherwise have vanished into the imperial cult.
subtitle: the Taiho Code, the Wado Kaichin coinage, and the move to Heijo-kyo

title: Nara Buddhism and the Great Buddha
bullets: The 735–737 smallpox epidemic killed roughly one-third of the Japanese population — somewhere between 1.5 and 2.5 million people — and killed all four Fujiwara brothers within a single year, breaking their generation's grip on Nara politics.

Emperor Shomu's response to the epidemic was to double down on state Buddhism: the Provincial Temple Edict of 741 ordered every province to build a kokubunji monastery with a seven-story pagoda and a parallel kokubunniji nunnery.

The Todai-ji Daibutsu, dedicated in 752, was a 49-foot bronze Vairocana cast in eight stages from over 500 tons of bronze plus the gold that consumed most of Japan's reserves; the mercury-amalgam gilding process killed or seriously poisoned many of the workmen.

The 752 dedication ceremony drew over 10,000 monks plus musicians and dancers from China, Korea, India, and Central Asia; the eye-opening was performed by an Indian monk named Bodhisena who had traveled from India via Vietnam and China.

The Shoso-in log storehouse at Todai-ji has preserved Emperor Shomu's collection of over 9,000 objects in extraordinary condition for 1,250 years — Persian glass, Tang mirrors, Indian musical instruments, Korean metalwork, all exhibited one month each autumn at the Nara National Museum.

The Six Schools of Nara Buddhism — Hosso, Kusha, Sanron, Jojitsu, Kegon, and Ritsu — were academic departments rather than missionary sects, designed to maintain a sophisticated scholarly establishment in the capital rather than to evangelize the rural population.

Ganjin (the Tang Chinese monk Jianzhen) attempted the voyage to Japan five times over eleven years, going blind from exposure during one failed crossing, before finally arriving in 753 to bring the proper Buddhist ordination lineage and found Toshodai-ji.

The Daibutsu was unveiled in 752, two years after the Abbasid Revolution; a year before the dedication, in 751, the Battle of Talas in Central Asia fixed the boundary between the Tang and Abbasid spheres and transmitted Chinese papermaking technology to the Arab world.
subtitle: the kokubunji network, the Daibutsu of Todai-ji, and Ganjin's voyage

title: Literature, Law, and the Dokyo Affair
bullets: O no Yasumaro completed the Kojiki in 712 from Hieda no Are's recitations — Japan's oldest surviving written work, opening with the divine couple Izanagi and Izanami churning the ocean to create the Japanese islands.

The Nihon Shoki, completed in 720 by a committee under Prince Toneri, was thirty volumes of classical-Chinese state history written for foreign-policy use, including alternative versions of many myths that the Kojiki simply harmonized away.

Both chronicles dated the founding of the imperial line to 660 BCE under the legendary Emperor Jimmu — almost certainly calculated by the eighth-century chroniclers to make the Yamato pedigree look at least as old as China's.

The Manyoshu of around 759 contains 4,516 poems by over 500 authors and uniquely includes anonymous frontier guards and farmers alongside emperors — the sakimori-uta border-guard laments are among the most affecting compositions in any pre-modern collection.

The Manyoshu was written in man'yogana — Chinese characters used purely for their phonetic values to spell out Japanese syllables, an absurdly inefficient system that would soon be simplified into the kana syllabaries of the Heian period.

The Yoro Code of 718 was a polished revision of the Taiho Code; promulgated in 757 under Empress Koken, it remained the operational legal framework of Japan for the next thousand years and was still being cited in Edo-period court rulings.

The Buddhist monk Dokyo, attending the ill retired Empress Koken in the early 760s, became her favored counselor and was given the unprecedented title Hoo ('Dharma King') in 766, with a 769 oracle from Usa Hachiman declaring he should be made emperor.

Wake no Kiyomaro brought back a counter-oracle from Usa blocking Dokyo's succession, and when Empress Shotoku died in 770 Dokyo was exiled — but female imperial succession was now politically suspect, and Japan would not have another reigning empress until 1629.

The Hyakumanto Darani of around 770 commissioned one million miniature wooden pagodas containing printed dharani charms — among the world's earliest surviving examples of woodblock printing, predating the Diamond Sutra of 868 by nearly a century.
subtitle: the Kojiki, Nihon Shoki, Manyoshu, and the monk who nearly took the throne

title: The Move to Heian
bullets: Emperor Kammu's mother was descended from a Baekje royal lineage that had fled to Japan in the seventh century — a fact discreetly noted in eighth-century chronicles and publicly acknowledged by Emperor Akihito in 2001.

Kammu's first capital move was to Nagaoka-kyo in 784; his chief minister Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated at the construction site in 785, his brother Crown Prince Sawara was implicated, exiled, and died en route — possibly by self-imposed starvation.

A series of plagues, floods, and family deaths through the late 780s and early 790s convinced the court that Sawara's angry spirit had cursed Nagaoka-kyo, and after only nine years of occupation Kammu abandoned it.

Kammu founded Heian-kyo ('Capital of Peace and Tranquility') in 794 on a geomantically auspicious site framed by mountains and rivers; it would remain the imperial capital for the next 1,074 years, until the Meiji Restoration moved the court to Tokyo in 1868.

The Emishi people of northeastern Honshu — descended from Jomon populations not fully absorbed by the Yayoi continental migration — defeated a Yamato army of 50,000 under Ki no Kosami at the Subuse River in 789, the worst Yamato military defeat of the period.

Sakanoue no Tamuramaro received the new title Sei-i Taishogun ('Barbarian-Subduing Great General') from Kammu — the title that would later be shortened to shogun and applied to the de facto rulers of Japan from the late twelfth century onward.

The Emishi wars exhausted the Taiho-Code conscript army system, leading Kammu to abolish conscription in 792 and replace it with professional soldiers recruited from local strongmen — the institutional foundation for the eventual rise of the warrior class.

The Asuka-Nara period produced the imperial line, the Buddhist establishment, the Tang-modeled state, the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the Manyoshu, the permanent capital tradition, and the deeper cultural pattern of selectively absorbing foreign material and remaking it into something distinctively Japanese.
subtitle: Emperor Kammu, the Emishi wars, and the founding of Kyoto
